title: The Honest Guide to Getting Plastic Surgery in Korea as a Foreigner
description: From choosing a clinic to flying home safely — a practical guide to navigating Korea plastic surgery as an English-speaking foreigner, without the glossy brochure version.
author: SM Plastic Surgery
content:
## The Honest Guide to Getting Plastic Surgery in Korea as a Foreigner

Korea has one of the highest per-capita rates of plastic surgery in the world. Gangnam alone has hundreds of clinics within walking distance of each other. The ecosystem exists. The question isn't whether you can get surgery done here — it's how to do it in a way that doesn't go sideways.

This guide is for people who are serious about it. Not the aspirational stage, but the actual planning stage.

---

### The language barrier is more complicated than it sounds

Most clinics will tell you they speak English. And many do — to a degree that's fine for booking appointments and basic communication.

Medical communication is different. You need to accurately describe your goals, understand what the surgeon is recommending and why, ask questions about risks and recovery, and absorb post-operative instructions. When any of that gets lost in translation, the consequences aren't a slight inconvenience — they're a compromised result or a missed warning sign.

The practical question to ask any clinic you're evaluating: "Who specifically will be with me through consultation, surgery day, and follow-up — and what is their English proficiency?"

The answer matters more than you'd think. A dedicated English-speaking coordinator who stays with your case from first contact through post-op follow-up is a fundamentally different experience from a clinic where English communication happens ad hoc depending on who's available.

SM Plastic Surgery has English-speaking coordinators on staff as a standard part of the international patient process, not as an add-on. This exists because Dr. Lee Moo-young made a deliberate decision that foreign patients should be able to communicate precisely at every stage — not approximately.

---

### How to verify your surgeon is actually performing your surgery

Ghost surgery is the term for when the doctor you consulted and agreed to have operate on you is replaced — without your knowledge — by a different surgeon, often someone less experienced, while you're under anesthesia.

It's not a rumor or a forum exaggeration. It has been covered by Korean news outlets, resulted in criminal convictions, and led to regulatory action by Korean health authorities. It exists in Korea's plastic surgery sector, concentrated in high-volume commercial clinics.

This doesn't mean every clinic does this. Most don't. But verifying it requires more than taking a clinic's word for it.

Things you can do:

**Ask for written confirmation.** Request that the clinic confirm in writing (email is fine) that your consulting surgeon — named specifically — will personally perform your procedure.

**Ask about the operating room setup.** Who else is in the room? What role do they play? A reputable clinic won't be defensive about this question.

**Observe the consultation dynamic.** Does the surgeon engage directly with you, or are they largely absent during the consultation? Surgeons who perform their own procedures tend to be invested in understanding your anatomy and goals before the operation — not just approving a plan that staff assembled.

**Check operating schedules.** Some clinics run parallel operating rooms simultaneously. This isn't automatically a red flag, but it's worth understanding how your clinic schedules surgeries and whether concurrent procedures are possible.

SM Plastic Surgery's operating policy: Dr. Lee Moo-young directly performs every procedure. If you have questions about how to verify this, our coordinators will answer them directly.

---

### Choosing a clinic: what to look for and what to ignore

**Ignore:**

- The number of followers on Instagram. Engagement and surgical quality are not correlated.
- "Before and after" photos without patient context. Lighting, posture, and angle can make almost any result look impressive.
- Vague claims about awards or rankings. Korea has a large market for paid medical tourism rankings that carry no independent verification.
- Pressure tactics or time-limited discounts communicated before you've even had a consultation.

**Look for:**

- A surgeon you can research. Can you find their name, qualifications, and training background independently? Are they published? Do they have verifiable credentials?
- Clear communication about what the consultation involves and who conducts it.
- A clinic that lets you ask questions without rushing you toward a decision.
- Transparent process around surgery day — who's there, what the timeline is, how you're monitored post-op before discharge.
- Realistic aftercare communication. How do they handle international patients after they've returned home?

---

### How long to stay in Korea

The answer people want to hear is three or four days. The honest answer is longer.

For breast augmentation — which is one of the most commonly performed procedures among international patients — most surgeons recommend a stay of at minimum **7 to 10 days**. The first post-operative check typically happens around day 7. Travel clearance is given at that appointment, not before.

For more involved procedures, or for hybrid surgeries involving multiple operative sites, 10 to 14 days is a more reasonable planning figure.

Why does this matter for flying? Long-haul flights at altitude involve reduced oxygen levels, immobility, and pressure changes. For a body in early surgical recovery, these aren't trivial factors. Rushing the timeline to save on accommodation is the wrong trade-off.

Plan your trip honestly. Budget for a 10- to 12-day stay for most body procedures. Your surgeon will clear you when the healing process warrants it — not before.

---

### Accommodation and logistics

Seocho-gu and Gangnam-gu are well-served by hotels and short-term apartment rentals at various price points. Both are walkable and well-connected by metro. For patients who want to be close to the clinic during the first post-op days, staying within the Gangnam/Sinnonhyeon area is practical.

Some clinics facilitate accommodation through partnerships or recommendations. Some don't. Either is fine — this is something to clarify with your coordinator early, so you're not sorting accommodation logistics on top of surgery logistics the week before you arrive.

For the first two to three days post-surgery, your mobility will be limited. Ground-floor or elevator-access accommodation is worth prioritizing. So is proximity to a convenience store or grocery — you won't want to be walking far for meals during the early recovery days.

---

### Post-op follow-up from home

The most underrated part of the international patient experience is what happens after you fly home.

You'll have questions at day 12. At week three. Maybe at week six if something looks different than you expected. The quality of your clinic's follow-up communication — not just while you're in Seoul, but after you've left — matters.

Before you book, ask: "How do you handle post-op communication for international patients after they return home?" A clinic with a structured answer to this question — a coordinator who remains your point of contact, a surgeon who reviews photos sent remotely, a clear escalation path if something needs attention — is operating differently from one that offers vague reassurances.

SM Plastic Surgery's English coordinators remain available to international patients after discharge. Questions, photo reviews, and concerns are routed back to Dr. Lee as needed. This isn't exceptional — it should be standard — but it's worth confirming with any clinic you're evaluating.

---

### A note on pricing

Pricing for procedures at SM Plastic Surgery is provided after consultation, once a surgical plan is established. This is true for most reputable clinics — the cost depends on the procedure, implant choice, and surgical complexity, all of which are determined after the surgeon has assessed you.

Be cautious of clinics that publish flat-rate package prices without a prior consultation. Surgery isn't a flat-rate product, and pricing that doesn't account for individual anatomy is a signal worth noting.

Video consultations are available for international patients before they book travel.

---

### SM Plastic Surgery — the basics

SM Plastic Surgery is located at 435 Gangnam-daero, Seocho-gu, Seoul — a 5-minute walk from Sinnonhyeon Station, Exit 7. Dr. Lee Moo-young founded the clinic in 2013 and has operated from the same location since. He trained at Seoul National University College of Medicine and completed residency at Asan Medical Center.

The clinic is not a high-volume commercial operation. It's a single-surgeon practice, which means every patient's case is managed by one person with full continuity from consultation through surgery through follow-up.

For international patients, that consistency — knowing exactly who you're dealing with, at every stage — is the point.

*To request a consultation, contact SM Plastic Surgery through the inquiry form at smpsjp.com/en.*
